Sutter.title: Impatience and Uncertainty: Experimental Decisions Predict Adolescents' Field Behavior
Sutter.abstract: We study the relationship between experimental measures of time preferences and uncertainty attitudes on the one hand and field behavior of adolescents on the other hand. Using a sample of 661 children and teenagers, we find that experimental measures of impatience and risk-seeking predict field behavior such as saving, health behavior, and scholastic effort. Time preferences are particularly powerful predictors, explaining variation in smoking, drinking, BMI, and savings. Experimental risk attitudes are linked to smoking and drinking. Gender differences in field behavior can partly be traced back to differences in time and risk preferences.

Fehr.title: A Theory of Fairness, Competition, and Cooperation
Fehr.abstract: There is strong evidence that people exploit their bargaining power in competitive markets but not in bilateral bargaining situations. There is also strong evidence that people exploit free-riding opportunities in voluntary cooperation games. Yet, when they are given the opportunity to punish free riders, stable cooperation is maintained, although punishment is costly for those who punish. This paper asks whether there is a simple common principle that can explain this puzzling evidence. We show that if some people care about equity the puzzling evidence can be explained in a coherent framework. The model makes several testable predictions.

Thaler.title: Toward a Positive Theory of Consumer Choice
Thaler.journal: Journal of Economic Behavior and Organization
Thaler.abstract: The economic theory of the consumer is a combination of positive and normative theories. Since it is based on a rational maximizing model it describes how consumers should choose, but it is alleged to also describe how they do choose. This paper argues that in certain well-defined situations many consumers act in a manner that is inconsistent with economic theory. In these situations economic theory will make systematic errors in predicting behavior. Evidence from psychology and economics is presented indicating the nature of these errors.

Kahneman.title: Prospect Theory: An Analysis of Decision under Risk
Kahneman.abstract: This paper presents a critique of expected utility theory as a descriptive model of decision making under risk, and develops an alternative model, called prospect theory. Choices among risky prospects exhibit several pervasive effects that are inconsistent with the basic tenets of utility theory. In particular, people underweight outcomes that are merely probable in comparison with outcomes that are obtained with certainty. This tendency, called the certainty effect, contributes to risk aversion in choices involving sure gains and to risk seeking in choices involving sure losses. We develop a value function with three properties: defined on deviations from a reference point, generally concave for gains and convex for losses, and steeper for losses than for gains.

Camerer.title: Neuroeconomics: How Neuroscience Can Inform Economics
Camerer.abstract: Neuroeconomics uses knowledge about brain mechanisms to inform economic analysis. The brain is composed of multiple systems which interact, with controlled systems (executive function) interrupting automatic ones, and emotions and cognition both guiding decisions. Neuroscientists use many tools including brain imaging, behavior of patients with localized brain lesions, animal behavior, and recording single neuron activity. The key insight for economics is that dual-process models are better rooted in neuroscientific fact, and more empirically accurate, than single-process models such as utility-maximization. This opens up the black box of the brain, much as organizational economics adds detail to the theory of the firm.

List.title: Field Experiments
List.abstract: Experimental economists are leaving the reservation by recruiting subjects in the field rather than in the classroom, using field goods rather than induced valuations, and using field context rather than abstract terminology in instructions. There is something methodologically fundamental behind this trend. Field experiments differ from laboratory experiments in many ways. Although it is tempting to view field experiments as simply less controlled variants of laboratory experiments, to do so would seriously mischaracterize them. What passes for control in laboratory experiments might in fact be precisely the opposite if it is artificial to the subject or context of the task. Field experiments serve as a useful bridge between data generated in the lab and empirical studies using naturally-occurring data.

Gneezy.title: When and Why Incentives (Don't) Work to Modify Behavior
Gneezy.abstract: Extrinsic incentives may come into conflict with other motivations. Monetary incentives from principals may change how tasks are perceived by agents, with negative effects on behavior. In other cases, incentives might have the desired effects in the short term, but they still weaken intrinsic motivations. To put it in concrete terms, an incentive for a child to learn to read might achieve that goal in the short term, but then be counterproductive as an incentive for students to enjoy reading and seek it out over their lifetimes. This paper examines the research literature on three important examples in which monetary incentives have been used to foster the desired behavior: education, contributions to public goods, and lifestyle changes regarding smoking and exercise.

Ariely.title: Predictably Irrational: The Hidden Forces That Shape Our Decisions
Ariely.abstract: MIT behavioral economist Dan Ariely refutes the common assumption that we behave in fundamentally rational ways. Blending everyday experience with groundbreaking research, Ariely explains how expectations, emotions, social norms, and other invisible, seemingly illogical forces skew our reasoning abilities. Not only do we make astonishingly simple mistakes every day, but we make the same types of mistakes. These misguided behaviors are neither random nor senseless. They are systematic and predictable, making us predictably irrational. We adhere to a certain level of honesty when it comes to cash. But nonmonetary abstractions can make us dishonest cheats. We may not take one dollar sitting on top of someones desk, but many will take one of their cokes from the breakroom fridge with few qualms.

Sunstein.title: Nudge: Improving Decisions about Health, Wealth, and Happiness
Sunstein.journal: Book (Yale University Press) / American Economic Review
Sunstein.abstract: A nudge is any form of choice architecture that alters peoples behavior in a predictable way without restricting options or significantly changing their economic incentives. To count as a mere nudge, the intervention must require minimal intervention and must be cheap. The oxymoronic term libertarian paternalism captures much of the thinking behind Nudge. Its recommendations are paternalistic in that they try to steer people in the right direction. But it is libertarian in that people are free to resist nudges if they choose to do so. Behavioral economists have established that people often make decisions that run counter to their best interests, producing what we describe as behavioral market failures. This soft paternalism operates without coercion and seeks to influence individuals to make better consumer and lifestyle choices.

Mullainathan.title: Scarcity: Why Having Too Little Means So Much
Mullainathan.abstract: Scarcity steals mental capacity wherever it occurs, from the hungry, to the lonely, to the time-strapped, to the poor. If the mind is focused on one thing, other abilities and skills including attention, self-control, and long-term planning often suffer. Like a computer running multiple programs, our mental processors begin to slow down. What we focus on is how the human mind functions when it perceives scarcity. There are predictable cognitive changes. The term is scarcity trap, and the basic idea is that our brains so tightly focus on what is so desperately lacking that thinking about anything else becomes tremendously difficult. Bandwidth refers to the limited number of things that any human can focus on at one time. Limited bandwidth and tunneling depletes self control and leads to impulsive behavior.

Autor.title: The China Shock: Learning from Labor Market Adjustment to Large Changes in Trade
Autor.abstract: Chinas emergence as a great economic power has induced an epochal shift in patterns of world trade. Simultaneously, it has challenged much of the received empirical wisdom about how labor markets adjust to trade shocks. Alongside the heralded consumer benefits of expanded trade are substantial adjustment costs and distributional consequences. These impacts are most visible in local labor markets where industries exposed to foreign competition are concentrated. Adjustment in local labor markets is remarkably slow, with wages and labor-force participation rates remaining depressed and unemployment rates remaining elevated for at least a full decade after the China trade shock commences. Exposed workers experience greater job churning and reduced lifetime income. At the national level, employment has fallen in U.S. industries more exposed to import competition, but offsetting employment gains in other industries have yet to materialize.

Shiller.title: Irrational Exuberance
Shiller.abstract: This book examines economic bubbles and is named after Federal Reserve Chairman Alan Greenspans famed comment about irrational exuberance warning of such a possible bubble. The term behavioral finance refers to research on financial markets that takes into account the details of human behavior, including human psychology and sociology. Psychologically driven volatility is an inherent characteristic of all asset markets. Home prices in some areas were six to nine times greater than median income, far above historical long-term averages. Home prices, when adjusted for inflation, have produced very modest returns of less than one percent per year when measured over long-term periods. The cyclically adjusted price earnings ratio, or the Shiller CAPE, has become a pillar of modern finance and grew out of research that earned the Nobel Prize in economics.
